name: Abadengo Lands Loop from Cerralbo
name_original: Tierras de Abadengo y Río Camaces — circular desde Cerralbo
description: This moderate 33.5-mile road cycling route from Cerralbo explores the Lands of Abadengo and Camaces River in Arribes del Duero Natural Park.
region_intro: Road cycling around Cerralbo offers a diverse landscape characterized by varied topography, rural charm, and historical sites. The region features a mix of rolling hills and flatter stretches, particularly along waterways and through fields. Cyclists can expect routes that traverse ancient settlements and offer glimpses into the area's pre-Roman past. This terrain provides a range of experiences for road cyclists seeking exploration. Best road cycling routes around Cerralbo The most popular road cycling route is Abadengo Lands Loop from Cerralbo, a 33.5 miles (53.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 24 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the rural landscapes surrounding Cerralbo. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is San Felices Castle and Lumbrales Loop, a moderate 41.0 miles (66.0 km) path. This route includes historical points of interest such as the Vetón Hillfort of Lumbrales. Local road cyclists also love the Lumbrales – Hinojosa de Duero loop from Somos lo que damos, a 50.8 miles (81.7 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 57 minutes. Road cycling around Cerralbo is defined by its varied topography, historical sites, and scenic rural fields. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, with 7 moderate and 3 difficult routes available. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 150 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cerralbo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Cerralbo?
A: There are 10 road cycling routes around Cerralbo, offering a mix of moderate and difficult options for exploration.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Cerralbo?
A: Road cycling around Cerralbo features a diverse landscape with varied topography, including rolling hills and flatter stretches, particularly along waterways and through fields. You'll encounter rural charm and historical sites, providing a range of experiences.

Q: Are there routes suitable for experienced road cyclists looking for a challenge?
A: Yes, Cerralbo offers 3 difficult road cycling routes. One challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20141252" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lumbrales – Hinojosa de Duero loop from Somos lo que damos&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 50.8 miles (81.7 km) with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes in Cerralbo for beginners?
A: Currently, the routes listed for road cycling around Cerralbo are categorized as moderate or difficult. There are no routes specifically designated as easy, suggesting the terrain might be more suited for cyclists with some experience.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see while road cycling in Cerralbo?
A: Many routes pass by significant historical sites. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027015425/san-felices-castle-and-lumbrales-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Felices Castle and Lumbrales Loop&lt;/a&gt; includes historical points of interest such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335377" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vetón Hillfort of Lumbrales (Las Merchanas)&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable sites in the region include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja&lt;/a&gt; and the town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5337189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lumbrales&lt;/a&gt; itself, with its Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción.

Q: Are there scenic routes with good viewpoints in Cerralbo?
A: The region's varied topography and rural landscapes naturally offer scenic views. Routes often traverse fields and follow waterways, providing pleasant vistas. While specific viewpoints aren't detailed, the historical hillforts like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335377" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vetón Hillfort of Lumbrales&lt;/a&gt; often occupy elevated positions, likely offering panoramic views of the surrounding area.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes around Cerralbo?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Cerralbo are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027636335/abadengo-lands-loop-from-cerralbo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abadengo Lands Loop from Cerralbo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027015425/san-felices-castle-and-lumbrales-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Felices Castle and Lumbrales Loop&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Cerralbo?
A: While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, regions with varied topography and historical sites like Cerralbo are generally best enjoyed during spring and autumn. These seasons typically offer milder temperatures and pleasant weather, ideal for outdoor activities like road cycling, avoiding the extremes of summer heat or winter cold.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Cerralbo?
A: Over 150 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cerralbo's varied terrain. They often praise the region's diverse topography, the charm of its rural landscapes, and the opportunity to discover historical sites along the routes.

Q: Can I find long-distance road cycling trails near Cerralbo?
A: Yes, Cerralbo offers several longer road cycling routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24539915" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hinojosa de Duero – Hinojosa de Duero loop from Yecla de Yeltes&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 54.9 miles (88.3 km) route, providing a good option for those seeking a longer ride.

Q: Are there any towns or settlements along the routes where I can stop for a break?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near local towns and settlements. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027015425/san-felices-castle-and-lumbrales-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Felices Castle and Lumbrales Loop&lt;/a&gt; goes through &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5337189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lumbrales&lt;/a&gt;, a town with cultural sites. Other settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446421" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo de Camaces&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5455585" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bogajo&lt;/a&gt; are also in the vicinity and could offer opportunities for breaks.
tips: Olmedo de Camaces takes its name from the former abundance of elm trees in the place as well as from its belonging to the Camaces field around the river of the same name. Until now, the only elm tree that gave its name to the town was found in the main square, which was felled by a tornado, taking away 250 years of existence of said tree.

The castle tower that still stands was part of the fortress built in the 13th century by the Portuguese king Don Dinis to protect the lands in the area.
It is advisable to visit the tower inside, it houses a Historical Interpretation Classroom and a permanent exhibition on the Border Fortifications. In addition, from the top floor you can enjoy beautiful views of the entire area, including neighboring Portuguese lands.

The city of Lumbrales is visited mainly for the ruins of Castro Vetón on its outskirts, but also in the urban area it offers several interesting places to visit. Among them, the Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción and the Verraco Vetón stand out in front of it and a few steps from the Plaza Mayor.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Lands of Abadengo and Camaces River circular route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Cerralbo. You should be able to find parking within the village, as it is a small, quiet community. Look for available spaces near the village center or on residential streets, ensuring you do not block any access points.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain in the Arribes del Duero Natural Park is characterized by a rugged, granite landscape with deep canyons. This means you can expect significant elevation changes, including substantial ascents and descents as the roads navigate the steep riverbanks and plateaus. While the roads are generally minor and quiet, some sections may have variable surface quality, including less-than-perfect asphalt. Cyclists should be prepared for an undulating route.

faqs.question: What points of interest can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route offers stunning natural landscapes and passes through traditional villages. You'll have picturesque views of the Camaces River and the unique flora of the natural park. Notable stops include the village of Olmedo de Camaces, the historic Castle of San Felices de los Gallegos, and the town of Lumbrales. The route also offers glimpses of olive groves, vineyards, and almond trees.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Arribes del Duero Natural Park is beautiful in spring when almond trees are in bloom, and in autumn when the foliage changes. Spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures, making them ideal for cycling. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be chilly, so plan accordingly and check the weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Is the Lands of Abadengo and Camaces River circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, it's important to keep them on a leash, especially when cycling, to ensure their safety and to protect local wildlife. Be mindful of the road surfaces and any potential traffic, even if minimal.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Arribes del Duero Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route within the Arribes del Duero Natural Park. However, as with any natural area, please respect the environment, stay on designated paths, and follow any local regulations.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is approximately 53.8 kilometers long with about 428 meters of elevation gain. For a racebike, it typically takes around 2 hours and 23 minutes of active cycling time. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or visit points of interest.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: Given the significant elevation changes and potentially variable road surfaces in the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, this route is classified as 'moderate' in difficulty. It is best suited for cyclists with good fitness and some experience with undulating terrain. Beginners might find the climbs challenging.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other local paths. You'll encounter parts of the R6 - Ruta de las Dehesas, R5 - Camino de la Estación de Olmedo-Cerralbo, and R3 - Camino Antiguo de Olmedo, among others, for short stretches.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a road cycling trip on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and terrain, it's advisable to pack essentials such as sufficient water and snacks, a repair kit for your bike (including spare tubes), appropriate cycling attire, sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses), and a fully charged phone. Due to the remote nature of some sections, consider carrying a basic first-aid kit.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food and drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through villages like Olmedo de Camaces and Lumbrales, where you may find small cafes or shops to refuel. However, services can be limited in this rural area, so it's always a good idea to carry enough provisions for your ride, especially water.

waypoints.name: Castle of San Felices de los Gallegos
